Title: Combining mutations at genes encoding key enzymes involved in starch synthesis affects the amylose content, carbohydrate allocation and hardness in the wheat grain

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: polymerizes ADP-glucose into largely linear α-1,4-glucan chains constituting amylose within the starch granule
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: branch-specific polymerization step for amylose formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point for amylose synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: triple null mutant combination of homoeologs in cv. Cadenza; reduced transcript abundance; starch granule protein profiling; metabolite/starch compositional analysis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: abolishes detectable amylose, maintains total starch, increases starch crystallinity and grain hardness, and alters soluble carbohydrate allocation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: up-regulated in Cad-SSIIa* and more strongly in Cad-SBEIIa*; strongly reduced in Cad-GBSSI*
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: field-grown developing grains sampled at 18 days postanthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Triple-null GBSSI mutant (Cad-GBSSI*) produced starch with no detectable amylose. 2. Loss of GBSSI did not reduce total starch but redirected carbon toward amylopectin and altered nonstarch carbohydrate composition. 3. GBSSI is the decisive enzyme for amylose synthesis in wheat endosperm.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: amylose proportion influences resistant starch formation and flour nutritional/technological properties
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: useful to generate waxy wheat with amylose-free starch for tailored processing functionality
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: increased grain hardness and altered viscosity, but no major penalty in grain weight or total starch
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: paper studies combined null mutations of all three homoeologs; background qPCR targets are not core genes here

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: amylopectin chain elongation branch in amyloplast
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: elongated amylopectin chains of intermediate length (approximately DP 11-22)
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: elongates short amylopectin glucan chains to medium-length chains during amylopectin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for proper amylopectin structure and indirectly determines amylose proportion and resistant starch
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point for starch composition and carbon allocation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: triple null mutant combination of homoeologs; absence of SSIIa isoforms in starch granule proteins; starch chain-length profiling; grain metabolite and phenotype analyses
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: raises amylose to 46% of total starch, increases resistant starch about 7-fold, reduces total starch and grain weight, and increases fructans, arabinoxylans and β-glucans
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: transcript abundance comparable to wild type in Cad-SSIIa* despite absence of protein isoforms
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: field-grown developing grains sampled at 18 days postanthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SSIIa null wheat accumulated amylose-rich starch (46%) with altered amylopectin chain distribution. 2. SSIIa deficiency caused lower total starch and grain weight but higher resistant starch and nonstarch carbohydrates. 3. SSIIa is a major determinant of endosperm carbon allocation and amylopectin architecture.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: higher resistant starch and associated health-linked carbohydrate enrichment may lower glycemic impact
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: valuable for producing nutritionally improved high-amylose wheat, though yield penalties must be managed
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: substantial decreases in grain weight and total starch, altered granule morphology, higher hardness
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SSIIa absence could not be inferred from transcript level alone; confirmed at starch granule protein level

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: branched amylopectin chains with α-1,6 linkages
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: introduces α-1,6 branch points into amylopectin glucan chains, generating intermediate branched structures
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: branch point-generating step in amylopectin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with/redirects flux relative to amylose accumulation by promoting amylopectin branching
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point and branch-switch enzyme for amylose:amylopectin ratio
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: triple null mutant combination of homoeologs; strongly reduced transcript abundance; amylopectin chain-length profiling; grain starch/resistant starch phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: raises amylose to 79% of total starch, increases resistant starch 36-fold, alters granule morphology and crystallinity, increases hardness, while maintaining grain weight and total starch
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: strongly reduced in Cad-SBEIIa*; loss is accompanied by up-regulation of GBSSI, SSI, SBEIIb, ISAI and ISAII
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: field-grown developing grains sampled at 18 days postanthesis
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: SBEIIb (transcriptionally compensatory, not direct physical interaction shown)
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SBEIIa null wheat produced very high-amylose starch (79%) and strongly elevated resistant starch. 2. Unlike SSIIa deficiency, SBEIIa deficiency did not markedly reduce grain weight or total starch. 3. SBEIIa is a highly effective target for improving resistant starch with limited yield penalty.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: elevated resistant starch is beneficial for low-glycemic and health-oriented food applications
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: particularly promising target for breeding high-resistant-starch wheat without major loss of grain weight
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: increased grain hardness and altered starch functionality/viscosity
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: mutagenesis of SBEIIa alone gave lower amylose than some RNAi lines likely because RNAi also suppresses SBEIIb; compensatory SBEIIb up-regulation observed
